graphs_figures.explanation: This figure illustrates the system architecture. It shows a Primary Base Station (PBS) connected to multiple Secondary Base Stations (SBSs), which in turn serve multiple users. The diagram highlights two access paths: users primarily access content from SBSs (solid line) and secondarily from PBS if not available at SBSs (dashed line). It also depicts the multi-agent learning framework, showing K user agents and N SBS agents, each with an 'Actor' (π) and a 'Critic' (Q) network. The 'Actor' takes state (S) and action (A) to produce a reward (R), while the 'Critic' evaluates the action. The training process involves global state (S_All) and global action (A_All) for the critics, while execution is decentralized.
graphs_figures.title: Fig. 1: System model

graphs_figures.explanation: This figure presents three sub-figures related to the simulation setup and initial results. Figure 2(a) is a scatter plot showing the geographical locations of the PBS, five SBSs (SBS1-SBS5), and three users (User1-User3) in a 2D coordinate system, visualizing the network topology. Figure 2(b) is a line graph showing the reward for each of the three users over 300,000 training episodes. All user rewards converge to a stable negative value, indicating that the learning process stabilizes and users achieve a consistent minimum delivery delay. Figure 2(c) is a line graph showing the reward for each of the five SBSs over 300,000 training episodes. All SBS rewards converge to stable positive values, demonstrating that SBSs successfully optimize their cache efficiency and revenue. SBS2 and SBS4 show slightly lower rewards, consistent with their farther location from users.
graphs_figures.title: Fig. 2: Locations and rewards of heterogeneous agents

graphs_figures.explanation: This figure shows two sub-figures illustrating the impact of SBS location and replacement cost on caching strategy. Figure 3(a) is a line graph showing the average cached fraction (λ) as a function of normalized content popularity for each of the five SBSs. It shows that more popular contents are cached by a larger fraction. SBS2 and SBS4, being farther from users, exhibit slightly different caching strategies, tending to cache the hottest contents more aggressively to compensate for their location disadvantage. The shaded area indicates the storage capacity limit. Figure 3(b) is a line graph showing the average cached fraction (λ) as a function of normalized content popularity for SBS1, under different replacement cost factors (β = 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.2). As β increases (higher replacement cost), the curves shift from up-concave to down-convex, indicating that SBSs prioritize caching more popular contents when the cost of replacement is higher or cache space is scarce.
graphs_figures.title: Fig. 3: Contents popularity vs. cached fraction

graphs_figures.explanation: This figure presents two sub-figures comparing the overall reward of users and SBSs across different MADDPG scenarios. Figure 4(a) is a line graph showing the average reward of all users over 300,000 training episodes for three scenarios: "Heter-MADDPG (users and SBSs)", "Homo-MADDPG (SBSs Only)", and "Homo-MADDPG (users Only)". The blue line (proposed heterogeneous MADDPG) achieves the highest and most stable negative reward (lowest delay), outperforming the homogeneous MADDPG variants. Figure 4(b) is a line graph showing the average reward of all SBSs over 300,000 training episodes for the same three scenarios. The blue line (proposed heterogeneous MADDPG) achieves the highest and most stable positive reward, indicating superior cache efficiency and revenue compared to the homogeneous MADDPG variants.
graphs_figures.title: Fig. 4: Comparison with homogeneous MADDPG

graphs_figures.explanation: This figure is a line graph comparing the global reward (sum of user and SBS rewards) of the proposed heterogeneous MADDPG with other DRL algorithms (DDPG, PPO, and TD3) over 300,000 training episodes. The proposed MADDPG (blue line) consistently achieves the highest global reward and converges faster and to a higher value than DDPG, PPO, and TD3, demonstrating its superior performance in balancing user delivery delay and SBS cache efficiency in a mixed cooperative-competitive environment. The total reward is improved by nearly 40% compared to homogeneous learning approaches.

limitations: The proposed approach was tested on a specific simulation configuration with a fixed number of SBSs and users, and specific geographical distribution.

The model assumes that a user can only retrieve one fragment of a file from a single SBS and the rest from the PBS, not from multiple SBSs cooperatively relaying fragments, which might limit the potential for more complex cooperative strategies.
summary.main_idea: The central concept is to propose a heterogeneous multi-agent deep deterministic policy gradient (MADDPG) approach for cooperative caching in Cognitive Radio Networks (CRNs). This approach treats both users and Secondary Base Stations (SBSs) as two distinct types of agents that learn to cooperate and compete to reduce user delivery delay and enhance SBS cache efficiency simultaneously. Key innovations include optimizing cached content fractions in a continuous space and leveraging centralized training with decentralized execution.
summary.overall_summary: This paper addresses the challenge of optimizing cache storage and content fetching in Cognitive Radio Networks (CRNs) where content popularity is unpredictable and primary user band occupation is dynamic. Existing DRL methods often treat the system as a single agent or homogeneous agents, overlooking the autonomous nature of users and cache servers, and are limited to discrete action spaces. The authors propose a novel heterogeneous Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG) approach. In this model, users and Secondary Base Stations (SBSs) are treated as distinct types of agents that learn to cooperate and compete. Users aim to minimize delivery delay, while SBSs optimize cache storage for maximum efficiency and revenue. The MADDPG framework allows SBSs to optimize the cached fraction of each content in a continuous space, offering greater flexibility than discrete eviction/retention. With centralized training and decentralized execution, agents can optimize actions individually based on partial observations, reducing communication overhead. Numerical simulations demonstrate that this heterogeneous MADDPG significantly reduces user delivery delay and enhances SBS cache efficiency, outperforming single-agent or homogeneous DRL approaches (like DDPG, PPO, TD3) by nearly 40% in total system reward, proving its effectiveness in balancing mixed cooperative-competitive objectives.
summary.problem_solved: This paper aims to solve several weaknesses and research gaps in previous cooperative caching systems for Cognitive Radio Networks (CRNs). Specifically, it addresses the difficulty of dynamically optimizing cache storage and content fetching strategies due to unpredictable content requirements and primary user band occupation. Previous DRL approaches either model multi-cache as homogeneous agents or the entire system as a single global agent, failing to account for the autonomous and often conflicting objectives of individual users and cache servers. Additionally, existing DRL methods like DQN often operate in discrete action spaces, limiting the flexibility of cache management. Centralized learning approaches also incur redundant communication overhead. The paper tackles how to maximize storage efficiency for cache nodes, how users can minimize delivery delay by selecting the best cache node, and how to design an effective learning algorithm for heterogeneous agents in a co-existing cooperation and competition environment.
summary.real_world_impact: This research has practical real-world applications in optimizing wireless network performance. By enabling distributed cooperative caching in Cognitive Radio Networks, it can significantly decrease network traffic loads, reduce content delivery delay for users, and free up valuable spectrum resources. This is particularly relevant for scenarios like edge AI, smart cities, and mobile broadband where efficient content delivery and spectrum utilization are critical. The proposed approach can lead to more responsive and efficient wireless communication systems, improving user experience and network resource management in dynamic and unpredictable environments.
summary.results_conclusion: The numerical simulations demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed heterogeneous MADDPG approach. User and SBS rewards consistently converge, confirming the algorithm's stability. The caching strategy adapts to SBS location and replacement costs, with farther SBSs prioritizing hotter content and higher replacement costs leading to more focused caching of popular content. Compared to homogeneous MADDPG variants, the heterogeneous MADDPG significantly improves both user rewards (lower delivery delay) and SBS rewards (higher cache efficiency). Furthermore, it outperforms other state-of-the-art DRL algorithms like DDPG, PPO, and TD3, achieving a nearly 40% improvement in total system reward. The final conclusion is that the heterogeneous MADDPG effectively balances the mixed cooperative-competitive relationship between users and SBSs, leading to simultaneous optimization of content storage and delivery delay, and achieving a better global reward in CRNs.

Cooperative Cache in Cognitive Radio Networks: A
Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Learning Approach
Ang Gao, Hengtong Liu, Yansu Hu, Wei Liang, Soon Xin Ng
Abstract—Deploying distributed cache in cognitive radio net-
works (CRNs), which spreads popular contents to the edge of
network during the off-peak time through spectrum sharing,
can reduce the deliver delay to users nearby without causing
severe interference to the primary network. However, due to
the un-predicable contents requirement as well as the band
occupation of primary users, it is non-trivial to optimize the cache
storage and contents fetching strategy of users dynamically. The
paper proposes a heterogeneous multi-agent deep deterministic
policy gradient (MADDPG) approach, which takes users and
cache servers as two different types of agents to learn the
cooperation and competition for mutual beneﬁts. The numeral
simulation demonstrates that comparing with the other single
or homogeneous deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approaches,
the proposed heterogeneous MADDPG can further reduce the
delivery delay of users and enhance the cache efﬁciency of SBSs.
Index Terms—Cognitive Radio Networks, Cooperative Cache,
Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient
I. INTRODUCTION
C
OGNITIVE radio networks (CRNs) provide a construc-
tive prospect for improving spectrum efﬁciency and
alleviating spectrum scarcity by allowing unlicensed secondary
network to opportunistically access the spectrum licensed to
primary network [1]. Cooperative cache, equipped at the sec-
ondary base-stations (SBSs) in CRNs [2] can spread contents
to the edge of primary network in exchange for additional
spectrum accessing opportunities. In speciﬁc, SBSs acting as
edge cache servers proactively fetch hot contents from the
primary base-station (PBS) during the off-peak time and serve
the users nearby through the secondary network, which can
alleviate the interference, decrease the trafﬁc loads, and reduce
the contents delivery delay [2]. As a result, mutual beneﬁts can
be gained by both the primary and secondary network.
The effective exploitation for edge cache highly depends
on the contents popularity. However, it changes dynamically
in both temporal and spatial which is unknown in advance
[3]. To this end, machine learning has been adopted to predict
the contents popularity based on the historical observation and
optimize the cache storage. For example, paper [4] models the
cache updating problem as a Markov decision process (MDP)
and takes the long short-term memory (LSTM) based deep
Q-learning network (DQN) to optimize the cache replacement

strategy in multiple small cells networks. It is a off-policy
based centralized learning approach that needs redundant
communication overhead. Paper [1] takes the multi-user multi-
cache contents delivery networks (CDNs) as a single global
agent, and optimizes both the cache storage and users’ contents
fetching strategy to minimize the system cost by LSTM based
deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG). Paper [5] also
adopts DDPG based approach to maximize the long-term
hit rate and minimize their front haul trafﬁc loads. Paper
[6] further employs the homology optimization to reform the
reward function to guarantee that the network parameters can
be efﬁciently updated during the training. Papers [5], [6] take
each cache node as an individual agent with a global critic
network to evaluate the cache action. However, they lose sight
that both users and cache servers are all autonomous agents,
which means users tends to fetch the content from the cache
node with the minimum delivery delay, while cache nodes
expect to storage popular contents to attract more users for
the maximum reputation or revenue [7].
The paper focuses on the following three issues by adapting
heterogeneous multi-agent DDPG (MADDPG) approach in
multi-cache CRNs. 1⃝For cache nodes, how many percentages
of a given content should be cached to maximize the storage
efﬁciency? 2⃝For users, which cache node is the best choice
for contents fetching to minimize the delivery delay? 3⃝How
to design an effective learning algorithm for heterogeneous
agents with different action and observation space in a coop-
eration and competition co-existing environment?
The main contributions of the paper are as follows:
• Different from the previous researches which take multi-
cache as homogeneous agents [5], [6], or model the whole
system as a single global agent [4], the paper proposes
a heterogeneous MADDPG approach, where both users
and SBSs are treated as two types of agents interacting
with each other, to reduce the average delivery delay of
users and enhance the cache efﬁciency of SBSs at the
same time.
• Unlike DQN[4] that only operates in a discrete action
space, by the proposed MADDPG, SBSs can optimize
the cached fraction of each content in an independent and
consecutive space rather than simply evicting or retaining
the whole content, which makes a ﬂexible usage of cache
and enhances the storage efﬁciency.
• With the centralized-training and decentralized-execution
features of MADDPG, the well-trained agents can op-
timize their action individually by partial observation,
i.e. the user fetches contents from SBSs leading to the
minimum delay, while SBSs optimize the storage strategy
to maximum the cache efﬁciency, which can reduce the
synchronization and communication overhead.

II. PROBLEM FORMULATION
A. System Model
As shown in Fig.1, there are one PBS that contains all
contents, and N = {1, · · · , N} SBSs as edge cache servers with
the caching size Cn (n ∈N), and K = {1, · · · , K} users1. Each
user/SBS is equipped with a single omni-directional antenna.
In general, users ﬁrst fetch a content (or part of it) from an
appropriate SBS by the secondary network. If the content (or
rest of it) is not stored in SBSs, users further access PBS by
the primary network. By the SBS-ﬁrst policy, the spectrum
resource of primary network can be efﬁciently freed up and
the average delay of the secondary network is decreased [3].
Thus, cooperatively caching popular contents in SBSs during
the off-peak time of the primary network can make mutual
beneﬁt for both the primary and secondary network.

V. CONCLUSION
Deploying distributed cooperative cache in CRNs, can
decrease the trafﬁc loads, reduce the delivery delay, and
free up the spectrum resource for other users. However,
the contents requirements are hard to predict. Besides, the
cooperation and competition among users and SBSs makes
the optimized problem more complex to be solved. DRL
provides an effective way to tackle the no-trivial issue. Unlike
other DRL approaches that only aim at learning either the
storage optimization of SBSs or the fetching action of users,
the paper proposes a heterogeneous MADDPG algorithm,
which novelly features at treating users and cache nodes as
two types of heterogeneous agents to learn how to cooperate
and compete spontaneously. The numeral results demonstrate
that comparing with other DRL approaches like homogeneous
MADRL, PPO, DDPG and TD3, the proposed MADDPG can
enhance the system reward by optimizing the content storage
of SBSs and delivery delay of users simultaneously.

However, due to the un-predicable contents requirement as well as the band occupation of primary users, it is non-trivial to optimize the cache storage and contents fetching strategy of users dynamically. The paper proposes a heterogeneous multi-agent deep deterministic policy gradient (MADDPG) approach, which takes users and cache servers as two different types of agents to learn the cooperation and competition for mutual beneﬁts. The numeral simulation demonstrates that comparing with the other single or homogeneous deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approaches, the proposed heterogeneous MADDPG can further reduce the delivery delay of users and enhance the cache efﬁciency of SBSs. INTRODUCTION C OGNITIVE radio networks (CRNs) provide a construc- tive prospect for improving spectrum efﬁciency and alleviating spectrum scarcity by allowing unlicensed secondary network to opportunistically access the spectrum licensed to primary network [1]. Cooperative cache, equipped at the sec- ondary base-stations (SBSs) in CRNs [2] can spread contents to the edge of primary network in exchange for additional spectrum accessing opportunities. In speciﬁc, SBSs acting as edge cache servers proactively fetch hot contents from the primary base-station (PBS) during the off-peak time and serve the users nearby through the secondary network, which can alleviate the interference, decrease the trafﬁc loads, and reduce the contents delivery delay [2]. As a result, mutual beneﬁts can be gained by both the primary and secondary network. The effective exploitation for edge cache highly depends on the contents popularity. However, it changes dynamically in both temporal and spatial

To this end, machine learning has been adopted to predict the contents popularity based on the historical observation and optimize the cache storage. However, they lose sight that both users and cache servers are all autonomous agents, which means users tends to fetch the content from the cache node with the minimum delivery delay, while cache nodes expect to storage popular contents to attract more users for the maximum reputation or revenue [7]. The paper focuses on the following three issues by adapting heterogeneous multi-agent DDPG (MADDPG) approach in multi-cache CRNs. 1⃝For cache nodes, how many percentages of a given content should be cached to maximize the storage efﬁciency? 2⃝For users, which cache node is the best choice for contents fetching to minimize the delivery delay? 3⃝How to design an effective learning algorithm for heterogeneous agents with different action and observation space in a coop- eration and competition co-existing environment? The main contributions of the paper are as follows: • Different from the previous researches which take multi- cache as homogeneous agents [5], [6], or model the whole system as a single global agent [4], the paper proposes a heterogeneous MADDPG approach, where both users and SBSs are treated as two types of agents interacting with each other, to reduce the average delivery delay of users and enhance the cache efﬁciency of SBSs at the same time. • Unlike DQN[4] that only operates in a discrete action space, by the proposed MADDPG, SBSs can optimize the cached fraction of each content in an independent and consecutive space rather than simply evicting or retaining the whole content, which makes a ﬂexible usage of cache and enhances the storage efﬁciency. • With the centralized-training and decentralized-execution features of MADDPG, the well-trained agents can op- timize their action individually by partial observation, i.e. the user fetches contents from SBSs leading to the minimum delay, while SBSs optimize

enhances the storage efﬁciency. • With the centralized-training and decentralized-execution features of MADDPG, the well-trained agents can op- timize their action individually by partial observation, i.e. the user fetches contents from SBSs leading to the minimum delay, while SBSs optimize the storage strategy to maximum the cache efﬁciency, which can reduce the synchronization and communication overhead.

Heterogeneous MADDPG is enforced by centralized train- ing (red and green data ﬂow) and decentralized execution (green data ﬂow) in Fig.1. Each agent has an actor and a critic to ensure the learning stability and overcome the overoptimistic in large-scale problem [13]. During the training, the critic collects the global observation SAll = [Sn n∈N : Sk k∈K] and the global action AAll = [An n∈N : Ak k∈K] to evaluate the ﬁtness An of SBSs or Ak of users, and generate the policy gradient to guide the evolution of actor network 2. During the execution, the well-trained actor makes the optimal action (An or Ak) independently without referring to other agents’ action or state, which leads to a less communication overhead. MADDPG further adds noise N to explore better strategies. Another essential technique is the experience replay buffer (RB). Each agent is equipped with a RB to store the (state, action, reward, next state) transition (SAll t , AAll t , Rk t , SAll t+1), which will be fetched out randomly to update the parameters. CONCLUSION Deploying distributed cooperative cache in CRNs, can decrease the trafﬁc loads, reduce the delivery delay, and free up the spectrum resource for other users. However, the contents requirements are hard to predict. Besides, the cooperation and competition among users and SBSs makes the optimized problem more complex to be solved.
